story:
At the Blackman Estate, late summer draped itself over a glittering crowd while Anna Fitzgerald slipped through clusters of guests, refilling glasses and catching stray glances. Frederick Blackman—gold cufflinks, unlit cigarette, the rising star everyone watched—spun their favorite story: the night he found Anna, soaked and stranded off Beach Drive, and fell in love.

He cut the music, raised a glass, and dropped to one knee. “Marry me,” he said. It wasn’t a question.

She held out her hand; flashes burst; he kissed her hard on the tile. She smiled through tears and asked for a quieter celebration, just the two of them. He promised her anything.

Five days later, he brought her to a cabin outside Rapid City, the Badlands bruising the horizon, stormlight rolling over the prairie. Inside were his grandfather’s rifle over the mantel and, closer, a revolver on the kitchen counter—casual as a paperweight, placed where she couldn’t miss it. 

Anna proposed a liquid dinner—just whiskey and a game to pass the weather. They played Scrabble.

Frederick opened with PLAYED.

Anna laid GRACE.

He glanced at it, smiled as if it meant nothing, and reached for more tiles. His ears pinked.

Frederick played a word without looking up. Anna drew fresh tiles, studied them, and placed LILY.

He flinched.

Another of his turns passed in silence. Then Anna set down SARAH.

“A name isn’t a word,” he said, but the practiced calm wavered.

Anna took another sip. She had been listening for months. Sarah, a girlfriend from years back—suicide, opioid overdose, alone. Lily, a young campaign assistant—drowned in the Potomac after a late-night meeting. A tight election that broke his way when his opponent was found in bed with a seventeen-year-old from Lily’s hometown. Fred shrugged it all off until he didn’t.

His hand cracked across her face and sent her sprawling. “Four months and you think you know me?” he hissed. “I say jump, you say how high.”

She laughed, blood bright on her teeth. “You aren’t curious about Grace—the first name I played?”

He shifted, angling his body to keep her from the kitchen, from the revolver. Not protectively. Possessively.

Anna lifted her glass, steady enough. “Do you remember Myrtle Point,” she asked softly, “that nothing town where you ‘rescued’ me?”

He looked closer: the freckles. The dimples. The auburn hair dyed wheat-blonde every four weeks. The face he’d rewritten into a story he could tell at parties.

Realization settled hard.

“Who are you?”

“Grace had a sister,” she said. “You left a loose end when you covered up her murder.”

He’d called it an accident—drunk driving. Anna’s voice didn’t tremble. Grace was four years sober. She didn’t drive.

For a beat, the cabin went still except for rain worrying the windows. Then Fred’s mask slid off. He laughed, sharp and real.

He lunged for the counter.

Metal scraped. The revolver came up into his hand, the muzzle finding her chest like it had practiced the route.

She didn’t flinch. Her lungs already felt syrup-thick; her heartbeat had slowed into something deliberate and wrong. The laudanum she’d stolen, drop by drop from his locked medicine cabinet—always after he’d unlocked it, always after his fingers had touched the glass—was already working its way through her blood. By the time he understood who she was, the poison had already made the rest of the plan irreversible.

“Do you know why South Dakota?” she whispered.

He scoffed, still aiming. “Because you’re romantic? Because you wanted a view?”

“It wasn’t whim,” she said. “It’s close to the Badlands. Close to federal land. Close to a jurisdiction your donors can’t buy with a handshake and a phone call. There’s already a police cruiser taking the road up to the cabin.”

His smile twitched. “A twenty-two-year-old girl can’t touch me. Three deaths were accidents. Files get buried.”

Anna’s gaze drifted toward his luggage by the door. “No,” she said, the word soft as mercy. “But poisoning your twenty-two-year-old fiancée might.”

She let herself fold. The Waterford shattered when she hit, glass biting her side, blood mixing with whiskey on her tongue. Frederick jerked back like the sight of her on the floor offended him, then bolted for the phone—already assembling a clean story, already trimming his own guilt into something he could live with.

He didn’t notice what she’d arranged because he never noticed the work other people did.

Anna thought of the laudanum she’d pilfered from his medicine cabinet, week after week, until she had a lethal dose. The tincture she’d poured into her own drink, and the half-empty bottle now resting in Frederick’s luggage, his fingerprints still on the glass from every time he’d unlocked the cabinet and handled it. She thought of the bruises she’d inflicted on herself just before public outings, the whispered concerns she’d planted about Frederick’s erratic behavior. The call made to the district attorney's wife: "I feel like something is very wrong with him. One moment he's the amazing, perfect man I fell in love with, and the next he's a stranger. If I don't call you by seven at night every night, please ring for help."

The soft wail of a police cruiser rose in the distance.

Anna thought of Grace’s easy laugh—the one he had taken—and smiled as the sound of the cruiser grew closer.

Then she felt nothing.
